items.title: As someone who has long been interested, and at time even obsessed, with pre-WW1 French political, cultural and economic history, I have read a great ...
items.description_html:
As someone who has long been interested, and at time even obsessed, with pre-WW1 French political, cultural and economic history, I have read a great deal about the Dreyfus Affair and have been as bewildered as anyone else about the passions it unleashed.

In this very interesting essay, Ruth Harris argues that the Dreyfus Affair was the first major example of a modern culture war, in which facts and evidence never mattered so much as which side of an existential divide you were on, and about which we are so familiar today.

I am not sure I agree that it was the first such example. After all even in France the Panama Scandal and the Boulanger Affair preceded the Dreyfus Affair, and had very similar casts of characters, and the US has an even longer history of political battles becoming culture wars, for example the bank wars under Andrew Jackson.

Still, the lessons of the Dreyfus Affair, as Harris explains, are very relevant for today's interminable and seemingly-existential political conflicts, and explain why facts often become unimportant in these battles.
https://drb.ie/article/rereadings-2-dreyfus-returns/

items.title: SCMP: "In recent years, the central government has been trying to ease residency restrictions. However, analysts warned that local authorities might h...
items.description_html:
SCMP: "In recent years, the central government has been trying to ease residency restrictions. However, analysts warned that local authorities might have different priorities and be reluctant to implement the changes for financial reasons."

This, in a nutshell, is why rebalancing is so difficult. By limiting legal residence rights and by reducing schooling, pension, medical and other social transfers, the hukou system is part of the structural reasons for the very low household share of China's GDP.

This low household share, in turn, explains China's very low consumption, its manufacturing competitiveness, and its reliance on overinvestment, surging debt and trade surpluses to keep growth from dropping sharply.

So why not eliminate the hukou system, as Beijing has been promising for years? Almost everyone agrees this would be good both for Chinese workers and the Chinese economy.

But there is a problem. Transfers benefit certain sectors of the economy at the expense of other sectors, and if the former are important, or politically powerful, eliminating those transfers can be highly disruptive in the short term.

The transfers implicit in the hukou system have for decades benefitted businesses (by keeping wages low) and local governments (by reducing social expenditures), and in so doing have subsidized the increases in economic activity needed to meet Beijing's excessively high GDP growth targets.

If eliminating the hukou means that local governments have to expand the services they now grant only to hukou residents, this expansion has to be financed, either by reducing overall services, taxing households, taxing businesses or (temporarily) expanding debt. It might also allow migrant workers to demand higher pay.

Because these all have significant political and/or economic costs in the short term , the best solution has been to talk about hukou reform but to implement it as slowly as possible in the hopes that something will turn up.

The hukou is only one of the many implicit and explicit transfer that keep the household share of China's economy very low, but they all suffer from the same implementation problem: raising the household share of GDP means reducing either the business share or the government share, and until you are willing to implement the latter, you can only talk about implementing the former.
https://sc.mp/otmw8?utm_source=twitter&utm_campaign=3359359&utm_medium=share_widget

items.description_html:
Dan Katz on the impact of AI on employment. I would add that productivity-enhancing technology is always a good thing as long as the growth in wages (which drive total demand) keep pace with the growth in productivity. In that case employment might shift from sectors with more productivity growth to sectors with less growth, but overall employment will be maintained.

items.description_html:
Bloomberg: Since Bretton Woods cemented the global dominance of the dollar in 1944, "Americans and the world have been arguing about it. Some US analysts worry about losing that status; a few think it’s not worth all the trouble. Plenty of non-Americans resent the dollar’s dominance; no one can put forward a better alternative."

Count me among those who thinks US dollar dominance is definitely not worth the trouble, but who also believe that its dominance will remain as long as the US is wiling (and credibly able), for political reasons, to embrace an overvalued dollar that sacrifices the interest of its manufacturers, workers and middle classes for the benefit of Wall Street, the US foreign affairs establishment, and mercantilist economies that need to acquire foreign assets to balance their trade surpluses.

items.title: This version of story confuses several things. The Plaza Accord did not "break" Japan's development model. That model was already broken once very hig...
items.description_html:
This version of story confuses several things. The Plaza Accord did not "break" Japan's development model. That model was already broken once very high investment rates no longer corresponded to the need for a massive rebuilding of infrastructure, housing stock and manufacturing capacity after the devastation of war.

Japan's growth model, which was the right growth model after the war when it was severely underinvested in nearly every sector, had made itself (through its very success) obsolete by the 1970s, as Albert Hirschman explained would happen, although, he warned, policymakers would find it extremely difficult to change the model, and so would likely go on to create a very different and opposite set of imbalances.

That is exactly what happened in Japan (and, indeed, in every country that followed the high-saving/high-investment development model), with the resulting overinvestment showing up clearly in the continued very-high investment share of GDP through the early 1990s (among the highest in history until China surpassed it in the mid 1990s) and, even more clearly, in the surge in the country's debt burden.

The latter is key, because when nearly all debt creation is used to fund investment, as was the case in Japan, the country's debt burden cannot rise if the investment is on average productive for the economy. The fact that it rose so rapidly for so long is strong evidence of massive investment misallocation.

That is why for years Tokyo had devalued its currency in real terms, which is just another way of saying that it forced up the value of the currencies of its trade partners. It did this to minimize the consequences of domestic overinvestment in manufacturing capacity (by exporting highly subsidized excess capacity) and to help mitigate the debt impact of its domestic overinvestment.

That is also why to say the problem was the "sudden" revaluation of the yen misses the point. For nearly two decades Washington (and policymakers in many other capitals) had asked Tokyo for a gradual revaluation of the yen (i.e. it asked Japan to stop forcing the dollar to be overvalued), but as Tokyo was unable to embrace any meaningful appreciation of the yen as long as it refused to take the necessary steps to rebalance its domestic economy, this left the US with only two options.

First, it could do nothing and suffer from the continued overvaluation of the dollar imposed on it by the Bank of Japan's currency policies. And second, because the US could not intervene directly in its currency the way Japan could, it could use the threat of tariffs (the standard alternative to currency devaluation) to force a near-term revaluation of the yen.

It chose the latter, but revaluation rebalances the domestic economy by transferring income from manufacturers (net exporters) to households (net importers) thereby raising the household share of GDP.

Tokyo, however, subsequently reversed this transfer by lowering interest rates and expanding investment lending, which transferred income back from households (net lenders to the banking system) to manufacturers (net borrowers).

That is almost certainly why the Plaza Accord were ultimately so much more painful for Japan than for Germany – not because the Plaza Accord created the problem, but rather because Germany never suffered from nearly the same extent of overinvestment as Japan, and began to adjust after the revaluation, whereas Tokyo tried to put off adjustment for several years longer by igniting an asset bubble and exacerbating the investment bubble.

Beijing is clearly determined to avoid the fate of Japan, but I worry that by misinterpreting what happened in Japan in the 1980s, it risks doing even worse than Japan did.


The China Academy: 1/7
Pettis and Setser are right that bad investment + debt matters. But this is a strawman. The Chinese Plaza lesson is not “one treaty cursed Japan.” It is: don’t let external pressure force a sudden currency move that breaks your developmental model.

Is the idea that someone would land at such an airport, be blown away by it, and then when he is stuck in the hell hole of the Indian city, inhaling poisonous air, he is going to think how fine the airport was?

What is the point of brand-building exercises when anyone who lands here can see within five minutes that things are a mess, we do not know the meaning of planning, and we are a danger to ourselves?"

items.title: At anchor in the Persian Gulf, Abhijit Chopra found out about the US-Iran peace deal when his phone lit up with messages from family and friends. The ...
items.description_html:
At anchor in the Persian Gulf, Abhijit Chopra found out about the US-Iran peace deal when his phone lit up with messages from family and friends. The captain of a crude oil tanker, he had to temper his excitement. There were no signs of celebration from nearby vessels, and no ships making haste towards the Strait of Hormuz.

Chopra and his 21-strong crew have been trapped since the war began in late February. At the beginning, they struggled with fear and uncertainty, which ebbed into boredom and a constant battle not to let negative thoughts set in. For more than 120 days they have waited, dining together and bonding by singing old Hindi songs at karaoke.

Full story
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-03/strait-of-hormuz-sailors-remain-trapped-despite-iran-us-ceasefire

items.title: "China was at India’s 2025 per capita GDP in 2007 and ranked 123. It has moved up to 73 and is on the verge of high-income status with a per capita i...
items.description_html:
"China was at India’s 2025 per capita GDP in 2007 and ranked 123. It has moved up to 73 and is on the verge of high-income status with a per capita income nearly five times that of India.

Vietnam was at our level in 2016 and is now ranked 122, with per capita GDP around 60% higher than India’s.

Can India reach high-income status? And by when?"

Good read by @neelkanthmishra
https://tessellatum.in/?p=625

"India is trying to cover the economic distance from low-income to high-income in about one-third the time it took the currently developed nations, but demographics are changing five to eight times faster.

Fertility is falling more rapidly than expected, and median age of the population may cross 40 well before the current projection of 2053.

Growth has been seen to slow substantially for countries after their median age crossed 40: the demographic dividend becomes a demographic tax as dependency ratios (number of dependents per worker) rise, labour force shrinks, and growth in productivity and demand slows."

items.title: "In June, foreign investors continued to aggressively sell EM equities, with net outflows of US$57bn, bringing YTD to US$144bn, the highest in more th...
items.description_html:
"In June, foreign investors continued to aggressively sell EM equities, with net outflows of US$57bn, bringing YTD to US$144bn, the highest in more than
a decade.

Outflows impacted almost every market (except small net inflows into Thailand), with especially strong outflows from Korea (US$30bn) and Taiwan (US$18bn), with outflows also accelerating for Indonesia and South Africa while the unwind of "Buy Brazil" trade continued.

The only good news was that the intensity of foreign selling in India decreased (US$3bn) and there were signs of some tentative return of China Southbound buyers."

- Viktor Shvets, Global Strategist, Macquarie

items.title: Few thoughts on Value added products here
items.description_html:
Few thoughts on Value added products here

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM 1.0. Union Cabinet approval is expected shortly. But the number is the smaller part of the story.

ISM 1.0 was a manufacturing-enablement scheme: get plants built, prove India can execute at scale and quality, put a 50% central capex subsidy behind fabs, OSAT and display units, disbursed pari-passu with capital deployment rather than tied to production targets like a PLI scheme.

That single design choice, government co-investing from day one instead of paying out after commissioning, is what let India move from approval to construction faster than most Western programmes.

And now ISM 2.0 is altogether a different story where it adds three new pillars on top of the manufacturing base: equipment and materials, IP and deeptech development, and talent at scale.

ECMS, the components and materials scheme, has been doubled to Rs 400bn. Basic customs duty has been removed on 25 critical minerals, lithium, cobalt, germanium among them, the first real move toward an indigenous materials base. On IP, the scheme is now explicitly targeting full-stack Indian chip design as a stated goal, DPIIT recognition for semiconductor startups has been extended to 20 years, and a datacentre tax holiday runs to 2047. On talent, the target is 85,000 industry-ready engineers by CY27, a workforce of process engineers, metrology specialists and cleanroom technicians that India's 3 lakh chip designers, excellent for GCC-style design work, simply cannot supply on their own. In short: ISM 1.0 proved India could build the box. ISM 2.0 is trying to make sure India doesn't have to import everything that goes inside it.

ISM was never trying to win at the leading edge; sub-7nm logic is absent from the roadmap and will stay absent for a long time. Instead it targets 28-110nm logic and OSAT, segments that together account for roughly 56% of global wafer capacity and cover almost everything India's own auto, industrial and consumer electronics actually consume.

India's cost base in OSAT runs an estimated ~45% below Taiwan on an indexed basis and ~10% below Malaysia, already one of the most competitive geographies globally.

Combine that with a design workforce that is roughly a fifth of the world's chip-design talent pool, second only to the US, and you get a genuinely differentiated entry point: cheap assembly, deep design talent, and a captive domestic demand base of $62bn today headed to $155bn by CY31 as electronics production scales toward the government's $500bn FY31 target.

Across design, India's presence is really two layers :- the GCC arm of global majors doing serious front-end work (Intel's ~$9bn design centre, Qualcomm's team that did the Feb'26 2nm tape-out, AMD's largest global design centre in Bengaluru, plus NXP, Nvidia, Marvell, Renesas, Texas Instruments) sitting alongside an early but genuine domestic fabless layer (Aura Semiconductor in analog/mixed-signal, Mindgrove in RISC-V SoCs, Saankhya Labs in SDR/5G broadcast backed by Tata's Tejas Networks, InCore Semiconductors spun off IIT Madras's SHAKTI processor programme, Silizium Circuits and Agnit in GaN, iVP Semiconductor in power discretes, Netrasemi in edge-AI chiplets);

In wafer fabrication India has exactly one project, the Tata Electronics-PSMC fab at Dholera targeting 28-110nm and first chip in Dec'26; OSAT/ATMP is where the actual manufacturing muscle is concentrated Micron's Sanand ATMP (DRAM/NAND, operational), Kaynes Semicon at Sanand (IPMs for EVs, operational), CG Power's CG Semi at Sanand (automotive/industrial packages, G1 live, G2 by end-CY26), Tata Electronics' Jagiroad OSAT in Assam (largest single OSAT bet, phase-1 commissioning), the HCL Group-Foxconn JV at Jewar (targeting CY28), plus a newer batch approved in Aug'25/May'26, 3D Glass Solutions and SiCSem in Odisha, Continental Device in Punjab, Advanced System in Package in Andhra Pradesh, Crystal Matrix and Suchi Semicon in Gujarat;

And upstream in equipment/materials, still the weakest layer, you've got Linde India, INOX Air Products and INOX India building out gas infrastructure at Dholera, Navin Fluorine and Gujarat Fluorochemicals moving into electronic-grade acids, Deepak Nitrite/Deepak Fertilisers into electronic-grade IPA, Aether Industries and Tatva Chintan and Jubilant Ingrevia into specialty semicon chemicals, Thermax/VA Tech Wabag/Praj Industries in ultrapure water systems, Carborundum Universal in fab-equipment ceramics, Voltas in cleanroom HVAC (already executing for Tata Electronics), and Siemens supplying automation into fab and OSAT plants.

Nearly all of this last group is still at the trial-order or MoU stage rather than committed fab contracts, which is precisely the gap ISM 2.0's new equipment-and-materials pillar is trying to force closed.

But this can be a interesting turn to Indian Semicon space in general

items.title: Why you should take notice when a management starts talking about Value Added Products? 1. Suppose you run a Dosa Tiffin shop and sell plain Dosa for ...
items.description_html:
Why you should take notice when a management starts talking about Value Added Products?

1. Suppose you run a Dosa Tiffin shop and sell plain Dosa for Rs 50. You are doing well and make a name for yourself. Customers start demanding Masala Dosa, Podi Ghee Dosa & Rava dosa from you. You include them in your Menu at a price point that is double of a Plain Masala Dosa. This leads to better realisations and higher margins for you.

In a nutshell you have released a value added product for your Tiffin shop :)

2. Just think of a manufacturing business which has different customers and they are expert at manufacturing Stainless Steel Pipes, and are mainly catering to chemical customers. Over a period of time, they realise that by getting more certifications & by putting more machines. They can easily increase the number of industries they cater to but only challenge is to go through audits to get the certificates & the time involved in setting up new machines.

They go through this cycle and start catering to higher barrier to entry industries like Nuclear, Data centres and Aerospace. This is called climbing the value chain and getting into adjacencies which ends up making your business more robust.

3. Whenever, a business starts talking about Value Added products. Always sit up and take notice. Let me give you a few examples-

A) In 2018 APL Apollo started talking about focusing on Value Added products like Heavy structural tubes. They were making an EBITDA per tonne of Rs 3000. Post introducing new value added products like Apollo Chaukhat, Steel planks, and DFT steel tubes. The EBITDA per tonne has increased from Rs 3000 to Rs5600 as of Q4FY26. This is how value added products add directly to EBITDA for a company.

B) Recently, Venus pipes which is a SS tubes manufacturer has won a LOI of 185 crores in Data Centre spooling. This is an example of how a company is going beyond the traditional industries it serves into something new where peers like Ratnamani in Nuclear spooling do margins upwards of 30% mark.

C) Another example is that of Goodluck India which has diversified away from core steel tubes business into manufacturing of 155mm Artillery shells. This is addition of a vertical where value addition and margins are much higher due to shortage of shells in the world. Base business makes margins of 8-10%, and new business will have margins north of 25%+.

D) A lot of Pharma CDMO businesses started out as API manufacturing and have steadily climbed the value chain to become Contract manufactures for innovators. Eg- Neulands margins went from 14-15% to 28% in this journey or how Laurus & Acutaas have steadily increased the mix of CDMO in their overall revenues. This journey of getting into a higher barrier to entry products is often full of lumpiness & volatility. This is something what the likes of AARTI Pharma or Granules are trying i.e. to increase the mix of CDMO in the overall business.

E) Finally, an example of a company where value addition can improve. Yash high voltage manufactures OIP and RIP bushings. In RIP bushings the realisations are 3-4x that of OIP bushings. Think of Bushings as a mission critical component used in transformers which constitutes 3% of the cost. Yash High is trying to backwards integrate into their biggest cost line item: Cores used for manufacturing Rip Bushings. This will be close to 10% of BOM for a RIP bushing. If they crack it, then the value addition per product will go up and import dependency will finish.

4. In conclusion:

A) Look out when managements are trying to enter higher barrier to entry product verticals.

B) Check if Gross Profit & Ebitda margins for a company have started improving, and look if the reason for the improvement is due to higher value addition or value added products.

C) Look out for words like: Value added products, climbing the value chain, higher margin products, new applications, backward integration etc in the concall. This will indicate if the business is looking to climb up the value chain or not :)

This is it for today. Hopefully, you loved the mental model of: Value added products.

Disclaimer: No recommendation to buy or sell. Pure idea is to teach you different mental models used in business analysis.

items.title: For decades India Defence story have been a manufacturing story where licence production, import substitution, domestic assembly have been the primary...
items.description_html:
For decades India Defence story have been a manufacturing story where licence production, import substitution, domestic assembly have been the primary talk of the town

But now the talks are incrementally moving towards new chapter i.e. who owns the IP and not just the factory.

This changes the economics you work upon where manufacturer competes on cost and capacity whereas for IP owner has to compete on R&D depth and gets paid for something that no one else can replicate easily.

New TAMs that are opening up across this theme in India are :-

Modern conflict is increasingly a contest of sensor-to-shooter loop speed and how fast you can detect, decide, and neutralize. Drones, AI-enabled autonomy, and electronic warfare compress that loop. Legacy platforms don't.

This is creating genuinely new addressable markets rather than just growing old ones:

> Tactical drones: procurement moving from ~INR30-35bn in the last cycle to INR120-140bn now
> Strategic drones: a separate ~INR300bn opportunity emerging alongside
> Counter-drone / anti-drone systems: multiple speakers independently flagged this as potentially as large as or larger than the offensive drone market itself
> Directed energy (high-power lasers, EMP): still nascent, but multiple players are already fielding real contracts (₹2bn-scale laser air-defense deals)

Nobody disputes that electronics is the value core of modern defense - C4ISR, software-defined radios, missile electronics, command-and-control. What's new is where the frontier of electronics is moving:

> AI-enabled mission software and autonomous decision systems
> Quantum computing - described as a national-level opportunity that could exceed 10bn in ecosystem investment
> Cybersecurity as its own dedicated vertical, not a bolt-on
> Space electronics as a genuinely new business line, not an adjacency

Semiconductors remain the biggest import dependency, accounting for the overwhelming majority of imported content even in otherwise highly indigenized electronics products.

Also now shipbuilding story is moving from defence to commercial shipbuilding. India pays ~US$85-100bn annually in freight costs, and roughly 85% of that goes to foreign shipping companies. Logistics costs sit at 13-14% of GDP. That's the actual policy driver and not military ambition, but plugging a massive structural outflow.

India wants to build the industrial base that captures value across the entire maritime chain >>> shipping, shipbuilding, finance, arbitration and not just build hulls.

And then there is this space economy that is emerging currently in India. India's space sector opened to private participation in 2020, the number of private space companies has gone from ~7 to over 300.

The military angle compounds it where the government's plan is for 51 military satellites represents less than 20% of India's estimated long-term requirement. Satellite constellations need a minimum of ~7 satellites for continuous coverage

On the commercial launch side, the economics are the story and reusability is the thing to watch. Current break-even launch costs sit around US$10,000/kg; recovery-enabled reusable systems are targeting below US$4,000/kg. That's the same cost curve logic that made reusability transformative in global launch markets, now playing out domestically, with genuinely indigenous technology (single-piece 3D-printed engines manufactured in one print cycle, for instance) rather than licensed tech.

Space is also quietly merging into defense electronics companies building satellite/space-grade electronics are the same companies building EW and radar systems. It's becoming one continuous capability stack rather than two separate industries.

items.title: On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM 1.0. Union Cabinet approv...
items.description_html:
On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM 1.0. Union Cabinet approval is expected shortly. But the number is the smaller part of the story.

ISM 1.0 was a manufacturing-enablement scheme: get plants built, prove India can execute at scale and quality, put a 50% central capex subsidy behind fabs, OSAT and display units, disbursed pari-passu with capital deployment rather than tied to production targets like a PLI scheme.

That single design choice, government co-investing from day one instead of paying out after commissioning, is what let India move from approval to construction faster than most Western programmes.

And now ISM 2.0 is altogether a different story where it adds three new pillars on top of the manufacturing base: equipment and materials, IP and deeptech development, and talent at scale.

ECMS, the components and materials scheme, has been doubled to Rs 400bn. Basic customs duty has been removed on 25 critical minerals, lithium, cobalt, germanium among them, the first real move toward an indigenous materials base. On IP, the scheme is now explicitly targeting full-stack Indian chip design as a stated goal, DPIIT recognition for semiconductor startups has been extended to 20 years, and a datacentre tax holiday runs to 2047. On talent, the target is 85,000 industry-ready engineers by CY27, a workforce of process engineers, metrology specialists and cleanroom technicians that India's 3 lakh chip designers, excellent for GCC-style design work, simply cannot supply on their own. In short: ISM 1.0 proved India could build the box. ISM 2.0 is trying to make sure India doesn't have to import everything that goes inside it.

ISM was never trying to win at the leading edge; sub-7nm logic is absent from the roadmap and will stay absent for a long time. Instead it targets 28-110nm logic and OSAT, segments that together account for roughly 56% of global wafer capacity and cover almost everything India's own auto, industrial and consumer electronics actually consume.

India's cost base in OSAT runs an estimated ~45% below Taiwan on an indexed basis and ~10% below Malaysia, already one of the most competitive geographies globally.

Combine that with a design workforce that is roughly a fifth of the world's chip-design talent pool, second only to the US, and you get a genuinely differentiated entry point: cheap assembly, deep design talent, and a captive domestic demand base of $62bn today headed to $155bn by CY31 as electronics production scales toward the government's $500bn FY31 target.

Across design, India's presence is really two layers :- the GCC arm of global majors doing serious front-end work (Intel's ~$9bn design centre, Qualcomm's team that did the Feb'26 2nm tape-out, AMD's largest global design centre in Bengaluru, plus NXP, Nvidia, Marvell, Renesas, Texas Instruments) sitting alongside an early but genuine domestic fabless layer (Aura Semiconductor in analog/mixed-signal, Mindgrove in RISC-V SoCs, Saankhya Labs in SDR/5G broadcast backed by Tata's Tejas Networks, InCore Semiconductors spun off IIT Madras's SHAKTI processor programme, Silizium Circuits and Agnit in GaN, iVP Semiconductor in power discretes, Netrasemi in edge-AI chiplets);

In wafer fabrication India has exactly one project, the Tata Electronics-PSMC fab at Dholera targeting 28-110nm and first chip in Dec'26; OSAT/ATMP is where the actual manufacturing muscle is concentrated Micron's Sanand ATMP (DRAM/NAND, operational), Kaynes Semicon at Sanand (IPMs for EVs, operational), CG Power's CG Semi at Sanand (automotive/industrial packages, G1 live, G2 by end-CY26), Tata Electronics' Jagiroad OSAT in Assam (largest single OSAT bet, phase-1 commissioning), the HCL Group-Foxconn JV at Jewar (targeting CY28), plus a newer batch approved in Aug'25/May'26, 3D Glass Solutions and SiCSem in Odisha, Continental Device in Punjab, Advanced System in Package in Andhra Pradesh, Crystal Matrix and Suchi Semicon in Gujarat;

And upstream in equipment/materials, still the weakest layer, you've got Linde India, INOX Air Products and INOX India building out gas infrastructure at Dholera, Navin Fluorine and Gujarat Fluorochemicals moving into electronic-grade acids, Deepak Nitrite/Deepak Fertilisers into electronic-grade IPA, Aether Industries and Tatva Chintan and Jubilant Ingrevia into specialty semicon chemicals, Thermax/VA Tech Wabag/Praj Industries in ultrapure water systems, Carborundum Universal in fab-equipment ceramics, Voltas in cleanroom HVAC (already executing for Tata Electronics), and Siemens supplying automation into fab and OSAT plants.

Nearly all of this last group is still at the trial-order or MoU stage rather than committed fab contracts, which is precisely the gap ISM 2.0's new equipment-and-materials pillar is trying to force closed.

But this can be a interesting turn to Indian Semicon space in general

items.title: Agentic routing is such an interesting study. Built a basic agent that does planning on Opus 4 but reasons on 4.6 through a tool call (skill call). Du...
items.description_html: Agentic routing is such an interesting study. Built a basic agent that does planning on Opus 4 but reasons on 4.6 through a tool call (skill call). Due to lack of document grounding, I embedded corpus into system prompt. Worked well. Second stage, enabled managed RAG for grounding & agent stated skipping tool call entirely, no skill calls. It treated grounded data as a higher evidence deterministic choice & skipped skill call. Tweaked tool description, still no skill call. Changed skill description matching to tool call & bingo!! Agent skipped grounding, entered planning mode & made a tool call. Evals for Agentic behavior is literally out paces everything in this field. What's your such key learning from a recent build?

items.title: A lot of defense to IT SIs right now is based on assumption that enterprise clients will not open their systems, data & processes to external AI solut...
items.description_html: A lot of defense to IT SIs right now is based on assumption that enterprise clients will not open their systems, data & processes to external AI solutions (Claude, OpenAi). However, this defense collapses the moment we have a precedence from a big enterprise opening up just the way enterprises opened up to cloud after a decade long friction. The moment this starts happening, the JVs OpenAi & Claude have formed will make a killing. And, what will take this wall down? For e.g., enterprises are currently very upset after SAP API policy that prohibited clients from using their own data & sending it to external tools via APIs to architect a solution outside of SAP. This can be the 1st hammer in the wall. One wrong step & clients will find ways to disconnect with enterprise OEMs & will be willing to trust(with an agreement perhaps) likes of Claude & OpenAi. The moment this happens, it is done!! This is the biggest risk I foresee.

items.title: My teachers gave me stock examples to explain investing frameworks. I took the logic, not the ideas. I adapted what fit me. Never cloned anyone. Never...
items.description_html:
My teachers gave me stock examples to explain investing frameworks. I took the logic, not the ideas. I adapted what fit me. Never cloned anyone. Never will.


Antifragile Thinking: My teachers gave me several stock examples as they explained various elements of investing frameworks. Understood logic. Never cloned any of their stock ideas. Took some elements from their framework, but added what was in-sync-with-my-inner-self-DNA. Never blindly clone anyone!

items.title: This framework wasn't built in a weekend. It is the product of decades of investing, countless mistakes, thousands of hours of reading, writing, think...
items.description_html:
This framework wasn't built in a weekend.

It is the product of decades of investing, countless mistakes, thousands of hours of reading, writing, thinking and refining my own mental models.

AI didn't invent the framework.

It helped me organise and express years of accumulated judgement into something practical, testable and continuously improvable.

I think of AI as infrastructure.

Like a road.

The road doesn't decide where you go.

The destination depends on who's driving, what vehicle they're driving, and the judgement they bring to every turn.

This framework is Version 5.0 today.

Hopefully, it will never be finished.

This is the site where our new 100 acre Integrated PCB mega campus will be built in 🇮🇳

PCB Fabs- Inspired by the way TI expanded semicon fabs in Dallas, we will be taking a similar approch here. There will be multiple Fab Units that will be built over time in this campus.

PCBA- we will have enough lines to support our customers scaling to larger numbers.

Components warehouse- a fully automated warehouse to ship components the same or next day.

R&D center- to research and build technologies related to manufacturing, not just pcb. Semicon and advanced applied engg.

I am dedicating my next 10 years building this campus and change the way electronics gets manufactured in 🇮🇳

#JaiHind

items.title: RT Sagar Awatade: I don't know who else to tell this to, so I am going to tell my story here. Every day is a struggle for a young business, but the la...
items.description_html:
RT Sagar Awatade
I don't know who else to tell this to, so I am going to tell my story here.

Every day is a struggle for a young business, but the last few months have been harder than usual.

We are a small Indian company. For more than ten years we have been building a homegrown brand in a product category dominated by big foreign players.

There are almost no Indian names in this space. We set out to be one.

We started in 2014. Over the years we began making parts in India instead of just importing, and we started selling in the US, Dubai, Nepal, Malaysia and South Africa.

We showed up at global trade fairs to represent an Indian brand on the world stage.

In 2023 we changed the import code we use for our product. We did not do this quietly. Every shipment was declared. Nothing was hidden. We didn't invent our approach.

We followed written professional advice and the way this product is treated in markets around the world.

And now we are facing a government demand running into tens of crores in duty recovery and penalties, plus personal penalties on the founders and even on an employee.

For a company our size, this is not a fine we can pay and move on from. This ends us.

We have not run from any of this. I am not built like that. It is not how I was raised. We have written to the authorities, met officials in person, and we have now filed a writ in the High Court.

All we are asking for is a fair treatment.

I set out to build in India and sell to the world. I am asking only that the system back honest founders trying to compete globally, instead of breaking them.

The process is the process, and it exists for a reason. But process should not feel like punishment.

From where I am standing today, it does.
I am not giving up. I have worked too hard for this. If you have read this far, please share it. If you know someone who can help, point them my way. Help me get the word out.

Sridhar ji highlights three very important areas in the podcast related to hardware which is also applicable for PCB manufacturing-

1. Speciality chemicals used in PCB.
We have been working closely with few R&D startups in this space- copper nanomaterials(Anuna Labs) and others. Its very important that we build this in India. Huge gap/opportunity.

2. CAD used in PCB- Spell from @PcbCupid is one step in this direction with a larger vision of building our own CAD tools for PCB. Team has made good progress on autorouting recently.

3. Test and Measurement Tools - Huge gap in this space and almostly entirely imported products today. TDR used in impedance measurment for example is a low hanging fruit in PCB space if someone wants to start.

All of these are important for building a strong electronics ecosystem in 🇮🇳


Sridhar Vembu: One company I hugely admire is NPCI, and every time I use UPI, I think of their work and the massive impact! So it is an honour to do the podcast with their CEO Shri Dilip Asbe. Thank you Dilip-ji🙏

items.title: Exactly 10 years ago in May 2016, i went to a session on bootstrapping at TiE event in Santa Clara. That changed my life. When i spent time in bay are...
items.description_html:
Exactly 10 years ago in May 2016, i went to a session on bootstrapping at TiE event in Santa Clara. That changed my life.

When i spent time in bay area working on advanced sensing technologies in semicon space, everything was there in life(money, granted patents, travelling to new country every month, wife, so called good life) but it still felt empty within.

Was burning a lot of midnight oil trying to build something, failed twice trying to build in US. Those failures hit hard and made me realise that everthing that i had was only beacuse of the company that i was working for, i was zero outside of it.

During this phase is when i first met @svembu ji, did not know who he was, did not know about zoho. He was wearing a plain simple green kurtha, jeans and slippers gving a session on bootstraping his company. I asked "can we bootstrap a hardware business too ?" And he said " why not ? "

This along with zoho story, made my conviction of moving back to India stronger, it took almost a week for me to digest what zoho was and what was built without raising a single dime from VCs and i felt so proud and blessed to have met the man behind.

Moved back to India same year in October. It was only about building something here which makes sense in long term, something which was ignored by people for a long time.

Ofcourse it came at a cost, divorce, hard time telling people not to worry about me or my personal life, making sure that my parents are not suffering, isolating all the peronal stuff with the vision of building strong electronics manufacutring ecosystem in India.

10 years FF- we are a strong team of 200+, bootstrapped, growing fast, building factories in India which can compete globally soon. On a personal level, i still hardly spend few thousand rupess everymonth, lead a minimal life, have a loving wife who belives in me and a kid who is growing fast, parents who are proud that i am doing something in India.

There is no multimillion dollars in my bank as safety, my kid and wife are Indians with Indian passport and my parents were teachers/avg govt employee without any background in business.

I dont feel empty inside me, anymore. Fire inside me is for building India's electronics manufacturing capacity that can compete globally, a strong ecosystem for next gen to build products from India and a strong team that can run this beyond me.

Jai Hind!🇮🇳

items.title: RT Anil Agarwal: It is painful to see India face the adverse consequences of a war we have nothing to do with, particularly because of raw material se...
items.description_html:
RT Anil Agarwal
It is painful to see India face the adverse consequences of a war we have nothing to do with, particularly because of raw material security. We don’t have to import 90% of our oil, or 95% of our copper or 99.5% of our gold or any other metal resources. Mother Earth has given us the best geology. Last 40 years of practice in the minerals, metals and oil industry have taught me this. Now is the time, with this positive government, to give entrepreneurs freedom.

At just 19, I came to Bombay from Bihar and built a company in this industry from scratch. Vedanta was able to acquire Hindustan Zinc and BALCO under a privatisation programme which was never completed. 26% and 49% stakes are still held by the government and not been transferred as per a predetermined agreement. Vedanta also acquired ONGC Oil and Gas asset via UK’s Cairn and Sesa Goa Iron ore from Japan’s Mitsui.

In each one, the ambition and goal was to increase the production so much that India wouldn’t need to import but, in fact export. In Hindustan Zinc and BALCO, we succeeded. We increased zinc production 10 times, 20 times in aluminium. In the process, more than 1,000 companies have come up for processing this raw material. And Vedanta has contributed Rs 4.5 lakh crore to the exchequer in the last ten years. In oil and gas, our vision is to produce 1 million barrels per day. In iron ore, my vision is to produce 100 million tonnes, 33% of today’s production level. There are many more underperforming government assets that can perform the same way.

Three or four decades ago, there was no know-how, no experts, no finance in this industry. It took a lot of determination. Vedanta raised 35 billion USD from abroad and invested in India. It brought experts and technology. Because of that, Hindustan Zinc can also produce silver and fertiliser, and we are working very hard to start producing critical minerals very soon, something we were told would never happen in India.

Globally, only few very large companies engage in mining as it is very difficult without scale. Vedanta should be for India what Rio Tinto and BHP are for Australia or Vale is for Brazil. Other entrepreneurs and companies must create new Vedantas.

Most of the advanced countries have become prosperous by developing natural resources, including oil and gas. In India, the system has to stop creating hurdles. It needs to take a long-term view, as this government is not revenue-minded but, production-minded. It must facilitate. Instead of notices and judicial activity, all businesses need to receive recognition and respect. Benefit of doubt should pass onto them.

In India, we have economic heroes who can raise production equal to the biggest and best in the world. We should not allow tunnel vision or outdated mindset to keep us small and dependent. Some may remember the case of Rio Tinto which had invested in a diamond mine in Madhya Pradesh. It could have made India a global hub for diamond production, but they exited because of a complex, interfering and discouraging system.

The most important thing is trust. Keep the system simple, keep regulation limited and you eliminate the root cause of wrongdoing. We should have self-certification instead of lengthy approvals and clearances. The Government issues the rulebook and entrepreneurs strictly comply, subject to audit. Constant enquiry and interference only discourage enterprise. Also, the mindset of discrimination between public sector and private sector should change.

This year my family had the biggest tragedy. It was heartening to hear the words of the Prime Minister who said, “You must stay strong and continue to do work which is important for India.” That is the motivation of my life. I have the blessings of my grandfather and grandmother, who spent 40 years in Vrindavan in a small hut, in Manav Seva Sangh with their guruji, Swami Sharnanand Ji Maharaj. They always advised me, if you have wealth, you must use it for society. If you don’t have wealth, you must always help society with your hand. We all are willing to work hard, take risks and ensure that India becomes self-sufficient. The system should encourage us to deliver for our society and nation; this will eradicate unemployment and encourage women empowerment. This sector has given the maximum revenue to the exchequer in India and in the world for nation-building. It will help in making our country a developed nation, as envisioned by our Prime Minister. Let’s make India & Indians proud.

items.title: Our entire country's 🇮🇳 production of bare pcb is equivalent of jlc capacity. If you take only multilayer, than its worse than this. Jlc is not ...
items.description_html:
Our entire country's 🇮🇳 production of bare pcb is equivalent of jlc capacity.
If you take only multilayer, than its worse than this.

Jlc is not even in the top 50 in 🇨🇳.

Let that sink in...

China was closed for 15days last month, we were overbooked by 3-4x and we couldn't get the machines for the bottleneck process on time.

Raw materials, capital goods everything has to come from 🇨🇳 and it takes time.

Factory in china can get these machines in 7 days to expand, we need to wait atleast 45-60 days.

What you see as an outcome is delays! Because most of the people serach for 🇮🇳 fab during Chinese New Year and Chinese equivalent of dushera season. Most of the fabs in India run overcapacity during this time.

We have grown by more than 3x this year and we remain focused on solving problems that we have,(delay being the major one)

We have also made great progress on our new 10x larger new plant and hope we handle the next season better.

Thanks for your support and patience. Always grateful 🙏

items.title: RT Hemant Mohapatra: Ok, so for anyone who's upset about the @SarvamAI models not beating GPT5/other 1T parameter models, my response: are you f kiddi...
items.description_html:
RT Hemant Mohapatra
Ok, so for anyone who's upset about the @SarvamAI models not beating GPT5/other 1T parameter models, my response: are you f kidding me? A year ago you were crying about India not having our own LLMs trained from scratch. The team has launched 14 amazing products in 14 days and it took years of work, each. And they span text, OCR, voice, LLMs, STT, hardware! I mean hardware! Edge models! Celebrate. They are just getting started. Sure, I am biased as their first investor, but we invested because we thought so highly of them. Of course we are biased. And the odds were enormous. But the opportunity is enormous-er :-) and these guys are absolute killers, and the team hunts! I haven't chatted with @pratykumar today but I heard someone say he saw that PPT for today's LLM launch session literally ON THE STAGE for the first time and laid it all out without a prompter. You can only do it when you know your material cold, and your heart is 100% living and breathing this stuff. Just incredible, congrats to everyone at @SarvamAI. Start of an era.

Today, one acre in the same KIABD area is 15cr!. Less than 5 years and there are still soo many vacant lands, not much industries running. But the prices are so high, that it's almost impossible for factories to be setup there.

Such artificial price hike up, mostly driven by government is not good for industrial growth!

We need to learn from China.

items.title: BSE JULY Volumes HIT so far post RBI circular - Equirius Note BSE volumes down 37% Against the first week of June'26 Post-RBI circular volume check (e...
items.description_html:
BSE JULY Volumes HIT so far post RBI circular - Equirius Note

BSE volumes down 37% Against the first week of June'26

Post-RBI circular volume check (effective 1-Jul'26): 1st two trading sessions on BSE

We reviewed BSE's initial volume trends following the RBI circular that took effect on 1st July 2026.

# 0DTE (expiry day) — 2nd July'26: Premium turnover came in at Rs 547.02bn, ~19% below the 1QFY27 average expiry-day turnover (Rs 679.18bn) and about 11% below the June'26 average (Rs 617.29bn). Against the 1st week of June'26 (Rs 716.80bn), it is ~24% lower.

# 1DTE (one day to expiry) — 1st July'26: Turnover stood at Rs 355.11bn, ~9% below the 1QFY27 average (Rs 389.49bn) and about 11% below the June'26 average (Rs 397.25bn). Against the first week of June'26 (Rs 561.28bn), it is ~37% lower.

While it is early trends, however, Jul'26 volumes have started on a much lower number as compared to Jun'26, thus posing risks for downward revision to estimates in premium ADTO.

items.title: P/B ratio has historically been a more reliable and stable valuation metric for predicting future returns compared to alternatives such as P/E or EV/E...
items.description_html:
P/B ratio has historically been a more reliable and stable valuation metric for predicting future returns compared to alternatives such as P/E or EV/EBITDA. I had demonstrated this through correlation analysis in an earlier investor memo.

The chart below tracks the P/B ratio (blue line) of the Nifty Smallcap 100 Index against the 25th percentile of its trailing 5-year P/B range (orange line). In simple terms, when the blue line falls below the orange line, the current valuation is in the lowest quartile of the past five years.

Since P/B is broadly proportional to ROE, the structural improvement in index ROE—from ~8–12% in the previous decade to ~15% currently—has led to a gradual upward shift in the orange line over time.

Notably, for the first time since the COVID crash, the current P/B has moved below this 25th percentile threshold. Historically, similar instances—during the 2011–2013 downturn, Q1 2016 correction, and the COVID crash—have been followed by strong returns over the subsequent 1–3 years.

This does not imply that markets have bottomed, nor does it guarantee short-term gains. In an event-driven environment, especially with geopolitical risks such as an escalation in war, markets could decline further by 10–20%.

However, what can be said with a reasonably high degree of confidence is that forward-looking returns over a 1–3 year horizon from such valuation levels have historically been better than average.

items.title: In my experience, this is probably the first time the Mid & Small cap indices have outperformed the Nifty 50 during a crisis time. It's also extremely...
items.description_html: In my experience, this is probably the first time the Mid & Small cap indices have outperformed the Nifty 50 during a crisis time. It's also extremely rare to see the Mid & Small cap indices (avg of the two) beating the Nifty 50 near the bottom of the cycle. Their avg CAGR has outperformed during the last two tough years as well as all the below trailing years. I am not that surprised as their balance sheet, P&L and cash flow quality has substantially improved post COVID. I believe the biggest multi-baggers will emerge from this space as the earnings growth differential will be significant going forward. Because of the quality differential today vs during pre-COVID period, I would be surprised if their valuations correct to levels seen during the previous crashes. We probably have the best corporate quality and least stressed financials currently compared to any other previous big crisis.

items.description_html: Earnings growth picture for Dec'25 quarter vs Dec'24 quarter. Wtd Avg (growth in sum total earnings) is significantly better for the mid and small cap universe. Small cap has an exceptional earnings from Kiri Ind with 5000 cr plus (vs 177 cr year ago) which skews the growth much higher than normal. If you remove that, the wtd avg growth is 14.9%. Median growth reduces influence of such outliers or exceptional items and hence gives a better directional picture. Over all growth is good but nothing exceptional. I believe we should see much improved growth in the Mar'26 quarter as sharp pick up in economic activity (as covered in my last memo) started in the month of December 2025.

items.title: As seen in the table from my latest memo, drawdowns in small caps during the January–March period—measured from the January peak to the February/Mar...
items.description_html:
As seen in the table from my latest memo, drawdowns in small caps during the January–March period—measured from the January peak to the February/March trough—are not an exception; they are a recurring feature of market cycles. Just as consistently, these phases have been followed by sharp recoveries once the market establishes a bottom in February or March.
Current sentiment is such that investors are overly focused on the negatives, making the environment appear so foggy that positives have become invisible. This fog, as always, will eventually lift.

While market bottoms and the depth of drawdowns can never be predicted, it is highly probable that market levels a few months from now will be disproportionately higher. Unfortunately, short-term declines are unsettling for investors, causing many to step aside and miss the eventual strong upswing.

In hindsight, this phase will look like an obvious opportunity. But while living through it, investors see only risks and landmines—never the runway for the next flight

items.title: Markets move through extremes on both sides. These extremes are largely driven by sentiment and, at times, reinforced by fundamentals. Currently, Indi...
items.description_html:
Markets move through extremes on both sides. These extremes are largely driven by sentiment and, at times, reinforced by fundamentals. Currently, Indian equities are experiencing extremely weak sentiment, driven primarily by disappointment over the US trade deal and subdued earnings growth over the past few quarters. However, the September 2025 quarter has shown encouraging signs of earnings growth.

The on-ground feedback I am receiving across various domestic businesses is very strong. For many companies, activity has picked up significantly this month. In my career, I have rarely seen such a wide divergence between market sentiment and on-ground business momentum. After a long time, deep value is emerging in several pockets, even though valuations at the overall market level are not cheap. A shift in sentiment will require a trigger. If not a trade deal, strong earnings and supportive management commentary could serve as a meaningful catalyst.

Wishing everyone a very Happy New Year and hoping for a far more rewarding 2026!

One has a 2 BHK Flat worth Rs 80L

Another has a similar priced 2 BHK flat

Personal finance is personal.

items.title: When Siddhartha Lal took charge of Royal Enfield, many people inside the industry were skeptical. Most CEOs would have treated the motorcycle division...
items.description_html:
When Siddhartha Lal took charge of Royal Enfield, many people inside the industry were skeptical.

Most CEOs would have treated the motorcycle division as a side project.

Instead, Lal did something unusual.

He became deeply obsessed with the motorcycles themselves.

He rode them personally.
He spoke directly with riders.
He studied what made people emotionally attached to the brand.

What he discovered shaped the company’s strategy.

Royal Enfield riders didn’t want:
the fastest bikes
the lightest bikes
the most technologically advanced bikes.

They wanted something much rarer. They wanted soul.

So Lal made a radical decision:

Royal Enfield would not compete with Japanese bikes on performance. Instead, it would build motorcycles around heritage, simplicity, and character.

This philosophy would later become known as “Pure Motorcycling.”

items.title: Picture this: It's 2005 in Hyderabad, the pharmaceutical capital of India. A former CTO who just watched his previous company get acquired for $550 mi...
items.description_html:
Picture this: It's 2005 in Hyderabad, the pharmaceutical capital of India. A former CTO who just watched his previous company get acquired for $550 million decides to bet everything on a radical idea—build a pharma company that treats African AIDS patients with the same quality standards as American cancer patients. No shortcuts. No "good enough for emerging markets." Just world-class chemistry at prices the developing world could afford.

That man was Dr. Satyanarayana Chava, and his company, Laurus Labs, would go on to become the world's largest third-party supplier of antiretroviral APIs, commanding 50% of the global market for key HIV drugs. Today, with a market cap north of ₹82,500 crore ($5.5 billion), Laurus has transformed from that single-minded API startup into an integrated pharmaceutical powerhouse spanning drug discovery to commercial manufacturing.

However, what makes this story remarkable is that, unlike the typical Indian pharma playbook of reverse-engineering expired patents and competing on price, Laurus built its empire on original chemistry.

Half of its 7000 employees work in R&D. The company holds over 300 patents. And in an industry where "Made in India" often meant a "cheaper alternative," Laurus supplies ingredients to nine of the ten largest generic pharmaceutical companies globally.

Src – empor top , no reco

items.description_html:
There's a reason why D2C brands are growing so rapidly in India.

Walk into many urban households today, and you'll notice the shift:

• Floor cleaners & home care: Legacy brands → Koparo
• Shampoo & personal care: Legacy brands → Plum
• Protein: Avvatar, PLIX, The Whole Truth
• Nutrition: Protein bars from Stroom, The Whole Truth & others
• Snacks: Protein & baked chips replacing traditional chips
• Instant meals: Health-focused noodles replacing conventional instant noodles

I feel these brands have two big advantages:

Thoughtful positioning - Non-toxic, plant-friendly home care (in Protein/fibre-rich foods - No palm oil, cleaner labels and better ingredients)

Quick commerce - Distribution is no longer a bottleneck. With 10–20 minute delivery, these brands now have mass reach and are just a tap away.

Indian consumers are increasingly paying for health, clean ingredients, and convenience—not just legacy brand names.

India's FMCG landscape is quietly undergoing a generational shift.

items.title: Rainfall shapes nearly every dimension of India's economy, ecology and food systems. Rain-fed agriculture occupies over half of India's cultivated lan...
items.description_html:
Rainfall shapes nearly every dimension of India's economy, ecology and food systems. Rain-fed agriculture occupies over half of India's cultivated land, making the timing and distribution of rainfall critical for agricultural production, rural livelihoods and national food security.

Unlike India's temperature trends, where we see a clear increase in average temperatures over time, the total annual rainfall across India has remained broadly stable since the early twentieth century.

On average, India receives between 1,000 and 1,300 mm of rainfall over the course of a year based on the all-India long-term average calculated over the period of 1901-2024. Over the past century, there have been considerable year-to-year fluctuations, but the cumulative annual rainfall has remained broadly stable, showing only a weak and statistically insignificant decline.

Rainfall data in India comes from observational records maintained by the India Meteorological Department (IMD). These datasets combine observations from thousands of rain gauges across India and extend back to 1901.

Despite this stability, rainfall is becoming more concentrated and erratic in several regions.

How often has India’s annual rainfall diverged from long-term “normal” levels? Read Juhi Chatterjee’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/long-term-rainfall/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Rainfall #Rain #Climate #India #DataForIndia

items.title: RT Madhav Chanchani: Another interesting point here is Peter Thiel's ability to read market cycles and knowing when not to invest. At the peak of the ...
items.description_html:
RT Madhav Chanchani
Another interesting point here is Peter Thiel's ability to read market cycles and knowing when not to invest.

At the peak of the 2021 bubble, he wanted the Founders Fund team to "go to the beach" for a year and stop investing.

But he knew the team would still want to deploy capital because the market was so hot.

So instead, he told them to identify the two or three best companies, invest at whatever the market-clearing price was, and ignore everything else.

One of those investments was Ramp, where Founders Fund invested at a $9 billion post-money valuation. Nine months later, Ramp had to raise a down round at $6 billion valuation.

But today, however, Ramp is worth around $40 billion - showing that backing good companies even if the timing is not ideal works out.

"I think there's something really unique about Peter. He understands that it's less important to be exactly right than to be directionally right, because it's really hard to shift the Overton window. Most people naturally moderate their views, and because of that they don't push hard enough to change strategy. Peter is different. He'll dig in his heels—not necessarily because he believes every word literally, but because he's trying to move the window, even if it's only a little. That small shift in thinking ends up being incredibly powerful," according to Trae Stephens of Founders Fund.


Madhav Chanchani: Partners from two top US venture firms, Benchmark and Founders Fund, say they are uncomfortable with the current funding frenzy because it reminds them of 2021.

“I’m very uncomfortable.....because it feels like we're getting back to a point where prices have become untethered

items.title: IT is up. Everyone is looking for a news or reason. Maybe it's just the realization that pessmism is running ahead of reality (tweeted this yesterday ...
items.description_html:
IT is up. Everyone is looking for a news or reason. Maybe it's just the realization that pessmism is running ahead of reality (tweeted this yesterday also).

Allied reading:
https://www.ndtvprofit.com/technology/palantir-ceo-karp-slams-anthropic-openai-token-pricing-something-has-gone-completely-wrong-11715871
Strong remarks made by Palantir CEO Alex Karp yesterday. criticized the token-based pricing approach used by AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, arguing that “something has gone completely wrong.” He said rising AI costs and newer, pricier model generations are frustrating enterprise customers, leading them to avoid “tokenmaxxing” (spending primarily based on tokens) and instead focus on clearer return on investment.

Karp suggested enterprises may shift toward open-weight models that can deliver similar capabilities at lower cost, and he highlighted concerns that China is advancing rapidly in AI development. He also emphasized a trend away from broad, general-purpose AI and toward more efficient, proprietary tools tailored to specific businesses.

In line with Palantir’s broader stance, Karp positioned open-weight approaches (including Palantir’s work with Nvidia) as a solution—framing the key need for buyers as control over compute, models, and data (the “means of production”), rather than handing that control to external providers.

items.title: What Chamath is saying below is not wrong, but it is nothing new - maybe 2 years late, and doesn't add to the discourse. Yes, the old model of T&M (bi...
items.description_html:
What Chamath is saying below is not wrong, but it is nothing new - maybe 2 years late, and doesn't add to the discourse. Yes, the old model of T&M (billing by the hour) is ending and shifting towards outcomes based pricing. The cost of new code generation is down by 80%, but it comes at its own cost (resources and community cost).

If the Enterprise world were to be built completely from scratch today, the "AI disrupts everything" argument would be true. As things stand today, there is a massive delta between how exponentially fast AI models are improving and how fast legacy multi-billion-dollar enterprises can meaningfully absorb them. This is creating a massive Enterprise deployment gap (refer image below from Infosys AI Investor day). This gap is a $300-400 Billion opportunity for Technology Services companies - albeit not IT players would benefit from this. There should be good value for IT players who can deliver the conversion layer with an AI-ready approach.

So far, the ROI for companies using AI instead of humans is not there and this may change in another year if the AI capex does not slow down. But as investors, at some point, we must ask - is the pessimism on IT running ahead of Enterprise reality?


Chamath Palihapitiya: Show me the incentive and I’ll show you the outcome.

The business model of Systems Integrators is to bill by the hour. You should not be surprised, then, when your project takes three years or more and is never finished.

items.title: Thought provoking talk on portfolio risk management. Mark Spitznagel of Universa Investments explains that dedicating 3% of a stock portfolio to true ...
items.description_html:
Thought provoking talk on portfolio risk management. Mark Spitznagel of Universa Investments explains that dedicating 3% of a stock portfolio to true "crash insurance" (deep OTM puts) boosts long-term compound growth better than relying on traditional "safe havens" like gold, Treasuries, or currencies, which often act as "imposters" by failing to deliver during actual crashes.

March 2008 - March 2020 period: A portfolio of 96.7% S&P 500 + 3.3% Universa tail-risk insurance delivered 11.5% annualized CAGR, versus 7.9% for 100% S&P 500 - a +3.6 percentage point improvement.

The 3.3% sleeve kept the overall portfolio essentially unscathed in March 2020 (when the S&P 500 fell 12.4%), while Universa itself returned +3,612% that month (+4,144% YTD).

Note: This is not a general recommendation.


0xDipper: Mark Spitznagel - founder of Universa, the "black swan" fund Nassim Taleb advises - on the most counterintuitive idea in investing:

add a 3% sliver of crash insurance to a stock portfolio. give that insurance a 0% expected return - it makes nothing over time.

items.title: RT West Bengal Diary: 🚄FROM VANDE BHARAT TO KOLKATA TRAMS: JUPITER WAGONS’ BIG BET ON BENGAL’S RAIL FUTURE!🚋 One of India’s leading railway m...
items.description_html:
RT West Bengal Diary
🚄FROM VANDE BHARAT TO KOLKATA TRAMS: JUPITER WAGONS’ BIG BET ON BENGAL’S RAIL FUTURE!🚋

One of India’s leading railway manufacturers, Jupiter Wagons, is making a major leap into passenger rail—and West Bengal is at the centre of its expansion plans.

🏭 New Hooghly coach factory: Jupiter Wagons will establish a dedicated manufacturing facility in Hooghly to build Vande Bharat trainsets, Metro coaches and other passenger rolling stock, making it Bengal’s second Vande Bharat coach manufacturing hub after Titagarh Rail Systems’ Uttarpara plant.

🚆 Entering the Vande Bharat era: The company is expanding beyond freight wagons to compete in India’s rapidly growing passenger rail market, targeting upcoming Vande Bharat, Metro and suburban train opportunities.

🌍 Global technology partnerships: Jupiter is working with international partners, including an existing collaboration with CAF (Spain), as it prepares to bid for major passenger rail projects, including Mumbai’s ₹20,000 crore suburban rail programme.

⚙️ Building the complete ecosystem: A new wheelset manufacturing facility in Odisha, developed with international partners, will supply critical wheels and axles for passenger coaches while also serving export markets.

🚋 Kolkata trams could benefit too: The company has expressed interest in participating in the modernisation of Kolkata’s historic tram network, should the state’s revival plans move ahead.

📈 Ambitious growth plans: Jupiter Wagons is targeting annual revenues of ₹10,000 crore over the next few years, with passenger rail expected to become a major growth driver.

If these plans materialise, West Bengal will strengthen its position as one of India’s leading railway manufacturing centres—producing everything from freight wagons and Metro coaches to Vande Bharat trains and potentially even modern trams.

#WestBengal #VandeBharat #Railways

items.description_html:
RT Press Trust of India
VIDEO | A remarkable piece of heritage has been restored in Karnataka's Belagavi district. A 300-year-old stepwell, which had been buried under garbage and debris, has been brought back to life.

The 80-feet-deep stepwell, believed to have been built during the Adil Shahi dynasty between the 15th and 17th century, has now been fully restored by the Belagavi-based Pyaas Foundation with the support of NSS volunteers and local residents. It reveals 53 stone steps, and a unique layout that looks like a Shiva Lingam when viewed from above.

#Belagavi #KarnatakaNews

(Full video available on PTI Videos - http://ptivideos.com)

items.title: RT VisionaryVoid: Nobody Could Explain Why They Were Hated. They Were Hated Anyway. For roughly 700 years, a group of people lived in southwestern Fra...
items.description_html:
RT VisionaryVoid
Nobody Could Explain Why They Were Hated. They Were Hated Anyway.

For roughly 700 years, a group of people lived in southwestern France and northern Spain called the Cagots. They spoke the same language as their neighbors, practiced the same Catholic faith, and were physically indistinguishable from the people around them. In 1880, a researcher who spent years studying the community concluded formally that he could find "no evidence which marks the Cagots out as a people distinct from the surrounding inhabitants." No different race, no different religion, no different language. Nothing.

They were still treated as untouchables. When they came to Mass, they entered through a separate side door, often designed to be intentionally low, forcing them to bow. They had their own holy water fonts; touching the shared one was forbidden. Priests served them communion at the end of long wooden spoons, to avoid skin contact. When they walked into a market town, they were required to shake a rattle to warn people of their approach, like a leper announcing himself. They wore a red badge in the shape of a goose's foot so no one would accidentally stand too close. They were banned from touching food in markets, from entering butchers and bakeries without announcement, from marrying outside their group. They were confined to ghettoes, called Cagoteries, usually built on the malarial side of the river. They were permitted to work as carpenters, masons, and rope-makers. In this capacity, they built many of the very churches that required them to enter through the servants' door. At some point in the 18th century, a wealthy Cagot in Brittany touched the normal holy water font. A soldier cut off his hand and nailed it to the church door.

Theories about their origin ran for centuries: descendants of Moorish soldiers, leftovers of the Visigoth invasions, heirs of Albigensian heretics, former lepers, members of a rival medieval woodworking guild whose enemies' contempt had hardened into law. None of them held up. The most recent serious theory, proposed in 2007 by British historian Graham Robb, is that they were simply a guild of skilled medieval craftsmen whose commercial rivals had successfully smeared them, and the smear ossified over five centuries into something that no one could any longer explain or undo.

The French Revolution abolished the legal distinction in 1789. Many Cagots raided local archives and destroyed every document that named them, erasing the paper trail of their own persecution. They assimilated and disappeared. Over 60 churches in the Pyrenees still have their separate Cagot entrances, bricked up now, partially obscured by paint and ivy. Nobody inside those churches ever really knew why they were built.

The most efficient prejudice is the kind that outlasts its own explanation.

items.title: RT SOIC Research: Today we are going to discuss a very unique 2W OEM that has been able to built a product that is built to last in 10+ years. For con...
items.description_html:
RT SOIC Research
Today we are going to discuss a very unique 2W OEM that has been able to built a product that is built to last in 10+ years. For context - They had no legacy nothing and have built the business ground up from nothing (product first distribution later)

Ather invested in the things that compound and are hard to copy i.e. design IP, software, brand, charging network and rented the things that don't (cell manufacturing). That is why it spends less than Ola yet has better unit economics, and why the moat is earned rather than bought.

And this is the fruits of investing in building technology and a superior product for years before just thinking about scaling distribution. At the end of the day we can see the result that the product won that did not catch fire :)

But here today we will get into details of how they created this MOAT and how it is now able to win a superior market share and show such a strong growth at the same time when competition did not decrease in fact it has gone up!

• No throttling, no gatekeeping, no one vendor deciding what science is okay.

Scientific AI should be open. One company shouldn't own the tools the rest of us discover with, or decide who gets to.

items.title: RT Jared Friedman: One of the great chapters in America's history is how we converted our economy to wartime production in the 1940s and used our indu...
items.description_html:
RT Jared Friedman
One of the great chapters in America's history is how we converted our economy to wartime production in the 1940s and used our industrial power to win WWII.

I'd learned in high school that we converted car factories into tank factories. But I never knew the full story until last month, when I read Freedom's Forge, the definitive book on this.

It turns out the true story is even crazier and more impressive. In honor of July 4th, here's the story of how America won WWII, one factory at a time.

items.title: RT Saurabh Chandra: It was a great show at Automate for @atirobotics. There is a clear vibe shift in robotics demand in US manufacturing. There is a h...
items.description_html:
RT Saurabh Chandra
It was a great show at Automate for @atirobotics. There is a clear vibe shift in robotics demand in US manufacturing. There is a huge intent to get robots now compared to earlier when people were evaluating robots.

While our pallet mover and Tugger were our workhorses with folks looking to deploy immediately, the show stealer was our industrial humanoid, Sherpa Mecha. Many people were surprised to see we did the actuators and had a very novel arm design (motors in shoulder, cable driven, carbon fiber arms) compared to most folks who have put together Chinese arms with software on top. All our products are made with first principles thinking and this is no different. Focused usecases and real deployments will win the market.

We will get to real deployments much faster (next summer) and beta deployments start this year itself. We already have a global customer in both US and India where we will be deploying the Mecha.

We also move into our new facility to assemble the robots near Detroit in July.

Never been a better time to be in robotics. Super excited!
